title: Exact noise characterization of entanglement distribution in star networks
authors: Kenneth Goodenough, Xiaonan Chen, Patrick Emonts
abstract: Multipartite entanglement forms the core of many networking applications. In the near-term future, it is expected that multipartite distribution will be achieved first through star topologies, making it important to understand the noise incurred during the distribution process. In such networks, elementary links are created stochastically and successful links must be stored while waiting for the remaining links, causing memory decoherence that depends on the random waiting times. We derive analytical expressions for both the average noise and its distribution, when distributing GHZ states under memory dephasing in star networks. We study and compare two distribution protocols: the factory and piecemaker protocol. Furthermore, we find expressions for the case of a global cut-off (allowing fast optimization of the cut-off without requiring Monte Carlo simulations) and extend the analysis for the factory protocol to depolarizing noise for arbitrary states.
summary: This work analyzes the noise accumulation when distributing multipartite entanglement over star network topologies. By comparing two protocols—Factory and Piece-maker—the authors derive exact analytical expressions for the average noise and its distribution under memory decoherence. The findings strongly advocate for the piececemaker protocol due to its superior performance in maintaining high fidelity across multiple users.
main_problem: The paper characterizes the noise incurred during multipartite entanglement distribution across star networks, focusing on how memory decoherence affects the final state fidelity.
main_result: It derives closed-form expressions showing that the piecemaker protocol significantly outperforms the factory protocol in terms of achievable users and key rates for a given level of noise.
method: The analysis uses analytical derivations, including generating functions and Pauli basis expansions, to calculate the average noise parameter E[Lambda] and its full probability distribution.
model_or_system: The system models quantum networking using star topologies where entangled states (like GHZ states) are distributed via a central node. The primary physical effect modeled is memory decoherence (dephasing or depolarizing noise) accumulated during stochastic waiting times.
key_observables: Average noise parameter E[Lambda], output fidelity F, and conference key agreement rate.
important_parameters: Decoherence rates ($\lambda$), link failure probability ($q$), number of users ($n$), and the global cut-off time ($T_c$).
main_assumptions: The analysis primarily focuses on memory decoherence; gate/measurement noise and initial state infidelities are ignored. The results often rely on approximations for finite cut-offs.
figures_summary: Figures compare fidelity vs. number of users (n) and key agreement rate vs. cut-off time ($T_c$) for both protocols, demonstrating the superiority of the piecemaker method.
paper_structure: The paper systematically derives noise metrics for two protocols (Factory and Piece-maker), extends the analysis from dephasing to depolarizing noise, calculates the full distribution of noise parameters, and compares performance using key rate metrics under finite cut-off conditions.
why_interesting: It provides rigorous analytical tools for quantifying resource degradation in quantum communication channels due to realistic decoherence mechanisms, which is crucial for designing fault-tolerant quantum networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Fidelity as a function of the number of end users n, for both the piecemaker and factory protocol. The solid and dashed lines correspond to the factory and piecemaker protocol, respectively. We plot the fidelity for several experimental parameters (λ, q) = (0.99, 0.6), (0.98, 0.7) and (0.9, 0.4).
FIG. 2: Conference-key agreement rate as a function of the cut-off time Tc for the factory and piecemaker protocol, for (λ, q, n) = (0.98, 0.9, 5) , (0.99, 0.95, 8) and dephasing noise.

FIG. 4: Distribution of the fidelity for the factory and piecemaker protocol. Parameters chosen are λ = 0.98, q = 0.6 and n = 3, 6, 9, corresponding to the solid, dashed and dotted lines, respectively.

FIG. 3: Conference-key agreement rate as a function of the cut-off time Tc for the factory protocol, for (λ, q) = (0.99, 0.85), n = 5, 6, 8, 10 and depolarizing noise. Note that for n ≥6 the use of a cut-off is necessary to generate key.

FIG. 5: Conference-key agreement rate with the factory protocol (top) and piecemaker protocol (bottom) as a function of the distance L, λ = 0.98 and for n = 5, 10, 15 end users. Solid lines are the case with binning (explained in the main text), dashed lines are without binning. The dotted, grey, vertical lines show the difference between factory and the piecemaker protocol.
